And so the financial planning community hates Suzy Orman and is constantly critical of her. This psychology community hates John Gray and is constantly critical of him, etc., etc., because of their oversimplification. To the point that the experts argue not only have they oversimplified, they've oversimplified to the point that it's erroneous. Make your own decisions about that, but it's a commonality to understand that influential writing is not about doing a better job of explaining things. Influential writing is about doing a better job of simplifying things, and one is not the same as the other. We're not really going for understanding, we're going for acceptance. Two different things. And then second thing, the commonality that the Suzy Orman story above all else points out, is they're all good at wrapping what they're doing in a feel-good wrapper. So that ultimately the person feels good about what they are hearing and reading about themselves as a result of it. Now they take them apart some, but they put them back together. And so those commonalities are there. I'm on page 31. Personal narratives have building blocks and sources. So when you think about your personal narrative as it exists now, and how it ought to be built, you want to understand that there are sources for it from life experience. And in many cases, people overlook the usefulness of all of their life experiences. I was interested, I think it was Skill, the Dean Coons interview. So is this a fictional author hyping his next novel that just hit the stores, which is a horror novel? A very black novel, I read a review. So he was on a radio for maybe 20 minutes today. 15 of the 20 minutes he was talking about his dogs, past and present. Now they somewhat have to do with the book because the plot of the book involves a dog. But that's not what they were talking about. They were talking about the dogs he had as a kid, the golden retriever he has now, the golden retriever rescue operation that he participates in. Well, why talk about that? And even if the interviewer wants to go there, why if you're the author trying to sell your horror novel, wouldn't you want to talk about the book? Well, because personal narrative sells better than product. That's why. And so I think it's Skill on his part. I don't think it was accident. It didn't sound accidental to me. He was busy working on connection rather than on pitch. And there's a lot more people who own dogs than like dogs than there are who read horror novels. And so there's a chance to get the fans and there's a chance to get some converts by what he was doing. So a lot of people overlook a lot of their personal stuff all the way back to childhood experiences that can be of real, real use to you. And you want to think about real common things. So like McCain has his POW war hero story. That's really only useful to him with military veterans. Now, that's a significant subset of the voting public, but it's not of great use to him anywhere else because people who haven't been in the military don't have any connection to it. The story you told me at breakfast about your father. See, that's a better story because more people have fathers than have military and military hero experience. So there's more common ground there to work with. But a lot of people don't tell their father stories or they tell one or two, but they don't have an arsenal of 20 or 30 or 40 that they use. And yet you're sitting on real value and power there that you aren't using. Most of those narratives along that line need to be, you know, struggled through some kind of personal problem or experience or tragedy usually or something. Well, no, not necessarily. They just need, and we're going to talk about the building of stories for purpose. They just need to have a punchline. They need to have purpose in a way that they are used, but they don't necessarily need to be a struggle story. So for example, you know, we're at the holidays right now, right? There's a shared experience among the majority of the U.S. population and actually most of the international population, right, is one way, shape, or form. Everybody gathers together for or tries to gather together or used to gather together. So everybody has the family gathering experience. So now you might have a story that really just has a warm feeling purpose to it that people connect to. Not necessarily a disaster recovery story, right? But even in testimonials, when you go to get testimonials from your customers, the testimonials are better when they're these kinds of stories. You know, if you go, see, like our best shed shop testimonial and the whole DVD is the woman talking about how she always wanted a dollhouse when she was a kid and her parents could never give her a dollhouse and now she's going to get a dollhouse. Now, it doesn't have a damn thing to do with how the shed's made or any of that, but see, that's a common, the commonality of that is there's a ton of people who wanted a bicycle and never got a bike. Glenn Turner tells this great story about, he's got a version of that story that he tells about, always wanting a new bike, you know, a pony, always wanting a pony. And never getting a pint every year. All he got is hand-me-down clothes from any one year. The kid that he gets hand-me-downs from had written a story to Santa to get a new bike and he copied his letter just exactly like that one and sent it off and he got a bike and he didn't get a bike. Now, there's a powerful punchline to the story, but the thing that makes the story work is so many people have some version of the same experience in their childhood. The dollhouse, bike, pony, and make a difference. They desperately wanted this thing and they were refused it, couldn't get it, didn't get it, right? So now we have a wide swath of connection, if you will, to build influence on, rather than a narrow swath of connection to build influence on. And personal narratives then have purpose, right? So that's the other thing. So we shall take both a coffee break and a first exercise break in a minute. So if you look at page 32, so personal narratives are built largely from the raw material of life experiences. And most people aren't super good at pulling all of that out on themselves. So in group, the object is to help each other find a useful story that they have not been using and telling, that you can help figure out a purpose for. I've given you a few tools. If you look at page 33, this is traditional writing stuff, by the way. You have what's called a heritage checklist. Now, there's lots of other places to go, but heritage is one of them. So you have a heritage checklist. On page 34, you have a partial, I have what I call my personal narrative asset list, which I keep adding to as I have new assets occur.
